title: Sheinbaum Presidency Update — Mar 29, 2026
summary: President Sheinbaum's approval rating fell to a presidency-low 54% per AtlasIntel/Bloomberg polling, driven by public anxiety over crime and economic uncertainty. Banxico surprised markets with a 25bp rate cut to 6.75% on March 26 despite inflation surging to 4.63% — the peso weakened to ~18.12 MXN/USD. The U.S. Supreme Court voided IEEPA tariffs and ordered ~$166 billion in refunds; the Trump administration substituted a 10% Section 122 tariff and launched new Section 301 probes against Mexico. Sheinbaum delivered historic water rights to Baja California, announced a National Water Law, and unveiled a General Law on femicide prevention. A Pemex refinery explosion at Dos Bocas triggered an oil spill and federal investigation. Estadio Azteca reopened as Estadio Banorte for World Cup 2026 with a Mexico-Portugal 0-0 friendly, while USMCA formal review talks advanced with the July 2026 deadline looming.

title: Sheinbaum Presidency Update — Mar 26, 2026
summary: President Claudia Sheinbaum defied U.S. pressure by reaffirming Mexico's Cuban medical worker agreement and backing Michelle Bachelet for UN Secretary-General, signaling an independent foreign policy posture. Mexico formally launched bilateral USMCA review talks with Washington on March 16, with Economy Minister Marcelo Ebrard meeting U.S. Trade Representative Jamieson Greer to negotiate on automotive, steel, and aluminum tariffs. Sheinbaum dispatched a humanitarian aid convoy to Cuba following a nationwide blackout, navigating a careful middle ground between solidarity with Havana and U.S. tariff threats. FIFA World Cup 2026 qualification playoffs opened in Guadalajara and Monterrey on March 26, a direct test of Mexico's security commitments following cartel retaliatory violence after the February killing of CJNG leader El Mencho. Sheinbaum continued pushing back against Trump's calls for U.S. military intervention on Mexican soil, urging Washington instead to address illegal gun exports and domestic drug demand driving cartel violence.
